pain_point_label: No Early Warning System for At-Risk Students & Families

matches.match_rationale: Student risk is fundamentally a structured classification problem: attendance records, grade trends, behavioral incident logs, sibling dropout flags, and family indicators (OFW status, rural address) are all tabular features that can be fed into a model to assign a risk tier (e.g., low/medium/high/critical) to each student. This directly addresses the core gap — schools have the raw data but lack the logic to combine signals into a flag. For a 500+ staff operator running multiple campuses, this can integrate with existing SIS/LMS data exports without requiring complex new infrastructure.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A student risk classifier that ingests weekly SIS exports and assigns each student a risk tier, surfaced as a dashboard for homeroom teachers and guidance counselors to prioritize follow-up. Could be trained on historical dropout data within 1-2 school years.

matches.match_rationale: Dropout is precisely the type of individual behavioral outcome propensity modeling is designed to predict — a probability score per student that they will disengage within the next 30-60 days, based on their behavioral trajectory. Unlike simple rule-based thresholds, propensity models can weight combinations of weak signals (a small grade dip + two absences + a behavioral note) that individually seem minor but together are predictive. This directly addresses the 'too late' problem by shifting the trigger from lagging indicators (already stopped showing up) to leading behavioral patterns.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A weekly dropout propensity score per student, with the top-N most at-risk students flagged for guidance counselor outreach before psychological withdrawal occurs. Model trained on longitudinal enrollment and attendance data from the operator's own campuses.

matches.match_rationale: Once a student is flagged as at-risk, the current process fails at the intervention execution layer — admin call once, get no answer, and close the file. A task automation agent can orchestrate the multi-step outreach workflow: triggering SMS/call attempts across multiple days, escalating to home visit scheduling if unanswered, routing to the right staff member (homeroom teacher vs. guidance counselor vs. social worker), and logging every touchpoint into a case record. This directly addresses the 'admin call once and close the file' failure mode by turning a passive, forgettable manual step into a persistent automated workflow.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An automated outreach agent that activates when a student crosses a risk threshold: sends sequenced parent notifications, schedules follow-up tasks for staff, escalates to case management if no contact is made within 3 days, and maintains a timestamped intervention log per student.

matches.match_rationale: Outreach to OFW families and rural parents must be personalized, culturally appropriate, and in the right language/register to prompt a response — a generic SMS blast is ignored. Text generation can produce individualized parent communication (SMS, letter, call script) tailored to the student's specific risk signals (e.g., 'We noticed Maria has missed 4 days this month and her math grade has dropped…') in Filipino, local dialect, or English as appropriate. This increases the likelihood of parent engagement at the first contact attempt, reducing the need for repeated escalations.
matches.opportunity_sketch: Auto-generated, personalized parent outreach messages triggered by the early warning system — each message references the student's specific situation, is language-matched to family preference, and includes a clear call-to-action for the parent to respond or schedule a call.

matches.match_rationale: Many OFW and rural parents are unreachable by phone but active on messaging apps (Facebook Messenger, Viber). A conversational agent deployed on these channels can initiate and maintain multi-turn dialogue with parents of at-risk students — asking about the student's situation at home, surfacing support options, and gathering information that feeds back into the case management system. This directly addresses the 'call once, get no answer, close the file' failure by creating an always-on, asynchronous outreach channel that works across time zones for OFW families.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A parent engagement chatbot on Messenger/Viber that proactively contacts families of flagged students, conducts a structured check-in conversation, captures responses, and escalates to a human counselor if distress signals are detected.

matches.match_rationale: Students who are drifting toward dropout often exhibit subtle behavioral deviations — attendance patterns that suddenly shift, a student who was consistently present becoming intermittently absent, grade trajectories that break from their historical baseline. Anomaly detection can flag these deviations without requiring predefined thresholds or labeled dropout data, which is valuable for a school that may not have enough historical dropout cases to train a supervised classifier. This is particularly useful for identifying the 'invisible' rural and OFW students whose disengagement is gradual and doesn't trigger any single hard rule.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An attendance and engagement anomaly detector that runs weekly on each student's longitudinal record, flagging statistically unusual changes in their personal baseline (not just absolute cutoffs) for counselor review.

matches.match_rationale: A complete early warning and intervention system requires multiple specialized functions working in sequence: a risk detection agent (monitors SIS data), a triage agent (prioritizes cases by severity and available staff capacity), an outreach agent (initiates parent contact), and a case management agent (tracks intervention progress and escalates stalled cases). No single agent pattern handles this end-to-end workflow — it requires orchestrated handoffs between specialized agents, each with different data access and tool permissions. For a multi-site operator, a multi-agent architecture also allows campus-level agents to report to a central oversight agent for network-wide visibility.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A networked early warning system built as a multi-agent pipeline: detection → triage → outreach → case tracking, with a supervisor agent providing network-wide dropout risk reporting to school leadership across all campuses.
